attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with six weeks of intermittent vaginal bleeding. Her last menstrual period was 12 weeks ago. She has a positive home pregnancy test but is concerned about the bleeding. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has had one previous uncomplicated pregnancy resulting in a live birth two years ago.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender with a uterus palpable at the umbilicus, which is larger than expected for dates. Speculum examination reveals a closed cervical os with minimal dark blood in the vaginal vault.

Transvaginal ultrasound shows a heterogeneous mass with multiple small cystic spaces giving a 'honeycomb' appearance, with no identifiable fetus or gestational sac. Serum beta-HCG is 225,000 IU/L (normal for 12 weeks gestation: 28,000-153,000).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the chromosomal composition of this condition?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features typical of a complete molar pregnancy: vaginal bleeding, larger than expected uterine size, characteristic ultrasound appearance, and markedly elevated beta-HCG levels. Understanding the genetics helps explain why no fetal development occurs.

46XX paternal chromosomes is the correct chromosomal composition of the most common type of complete molar pregnancy. It occurs when an empty ovum (containing no maternal chromosomes) is fertilised by a sperm. The paternal chromosomes then duplicate, resulting in 46 chromosomes, all of paternal origin. This explains why no fetal development occurs, as maternal genes are essential for normal embryonic development. More rarely a complete molar pregnancy can also have a genetic makeup of 46XY when an empty egg is fertilised by two sperm - again all from paternal genetic origin.

23X maternal and 23Y paternal chromosomes describes normal fertilisation leading to a viable pregnancy, which would show normal fetal development on ultrasound.

46XX maternal chromosomes would be incompatible with pregnancy as paternal genetic material is required for normal embryonic development and particularly for placental development.

69XXY triploidy is associated with partial molar pregnancy, where there are two sets of paternal chromosomes and one set of maternal chromosomes. These pregnancies may show some fetal development, unlike complete molar pregnancies.

92XXYY tetraploidy represents four sets of chromosomes, which is not typically associated with molar pregnancy and would not be viable.

Complete molar pregnancies require careful follow-up with serial beta-HCG measurements after evacuation, as they have a risk of developing into gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. The absence of maternal chromosomes and the presence of only paternal chromosomes explains the abnormal trophoblastic proliferation and absence of fetal development seen in these cases.
learningPoints: Complete molar pregnancy occurs when an empty ovum is fertilised by a sperm that duplicates its chromosomes, resulting in 46 chromosomes all of paternal origin.
Complete molar pregnancies typically present with vaginal bleeding, markedly elevated beta-HCG, and a characteristic 'grape-like' appearance on ultrasound.
The absence of maternal chromosomes in complete molar pregnancy explains the lack of fetal development.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Gestational Trophoblastic Disease (Green-top Guideline No. 38). London: RCOG; 2020 [cited 2024 Nov 24]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/gestational-trophoblastic-disease-green-top-guideline-no-38/
